name: Nan Madol Ruins
description: Nan Madol is an ancient city located off the coast of Pohnpei in Micronesia, consisting of a series of artificial islets connected by canals. Constructed from massive basalt columns, the site dates back to the Saudeleur Dynasty (c. 1100-1628 CE). The mainstream archaeological view attributes its construction to skilled Pohnpeian laborers using levers and rafts to move the heavy stones over centuries. Some alternative theories propose that Nan Madol was built by a lost civilization with advanced technology, citing the immense size and weight of the basalt columns as evidence that surpasses the capabilities of a 'primitive' society. These theories often involve claims of anti-gravity technology, lost knowledge from Atlantis or Lemuria, or even extraterrestrial intervention. Proponents point to the lack of readily available basalt quarries on the main island of Pohnpei as further evidence of extraordinary construction methods. Mainstream archaeology refutes these claims, asserting that the basalt was quarried from Sokehs Island and transported by sea on rafts. They also emphasize the evidence of a hierarchical society capable of organizing and sustaining a large workforce over generations to complete the project.
subtitle: Mysterious megalithic city of Pohnpei.

claims.claim: Nan Madol was built using advanced anti-gravity technology.
claims.evidence: The enormous weight of the basalt columns and the lack of suitable quarry sites nearby make traditional construction methods implausible.
claims.criticism: Archaeological evidence suggests basalt was quarried on Sokehs Island and transported by rafts. The weight, while significant, is manageable with sustained effort and a large workforce over time.

claims.claim: Nan Madol is a remnant of a lost civilization, possibly Atlantis or Lemuria.
claims.evidence: The sophisticated engineering and unique architecture suggest a level of knowledge beyond what is traditionally attributed to Micronesian cultures.
claims.criticism: There is no geological or archaeological evidence to support the existence of Atlantis or Lemuria. The architecture, while impressive, is consistent with the development of a complex society with skilled builders.

claims.claim: Extraterrestrial beings assisted in the construction of Nan Madol.
claims.evidence: The precision and scale of the construction are beyond the capabilities of humans at the time.
claims.criticism: There is no scientific evidence to support the involvement of extraterrestrial beings in the construction of Nan Madol. The site can be explained through human ingenuity and societal organization.

keyFigures.name: David Hatcher Childress
keyFigures.role: Author promoting alternative theories about Nan Madol's construction.

keyFigures.name: Patrick Nunn
keyFigures.role: Geographer and archaeologist researching coastal changes and settlement history in the Pacific.

mythologyConnections: Legends of magic and powerful sorcerers responsible for lifting the stones.
Stories of a hidden, underwater city beneath Nan Madol.

timeline.event: Beginning of construction under the Saudeleur Dynasty.
Decline of the Saudeleur Dynasty and abandonment of Nan Madol as a primary residence.
European explorers begin documenting and studying Nan Madol.
Nan Madol remains a subject of archaeological research and alternative theories.

sources.author: Childress, David Hatcher
sources.work: Lost Cities of Ancient Lemuria & the Pacific
sources.title: Childress, David Hatcher
